cases.passage: The United States' invasion of Iraq in 2003 was a pivotal moment in the exercise of its hegemony in world politics. The US government, under the leadership of President George W. Bush, claimed that Iraq possessed weapons of mass destruction, which posed a threat to global security. This claim was used to justify the invasion, which was widely opposed by the international community. The invasion led to a protracted and bloody conflict that resulted in the deaths of thousands of Iraqis and the displacement of millions. The US's actions were seen as a clear exercise of its military power and a demonstration of its willingness to use force to achieve its goals.

questions.q: What was the main reason cited by the US government for invading Iraq in 2003?
opts: (A) To overthrow Saddam Hussein
(B) To protect global security from weapons of mass destruction
(C) To expand US economic interests
(D) To establish a new government in Iraq
questions.explanation: The passage states that the US government claimed Iraq possessed weapons of mass destruction, which posed a threat to global security.

questions.q: How did the international community react to the US invasion of Iraq?
opts: (A) It widely supported the invasion
(B) It remained neutral
(C) It widely opposed the invasion
(D) It demanded a ceasefire
questions.explanation: The passage states that the invasion was 'widely opposed by the international community'.

questions.q: What was the outcome of the US invasion of Iraq?
opts: (A) A peaceful transition to democracy
(B) A protracted and bloody conflict
(C) The establishment of a new government in Iraq
(D) The defeat of Saddam Hussein's regime
questions.explanation: The passage states that the invasion led to a 'protracted and bloody conflict' that resulted in the deaths of thousands of Iraqis and the displacement of millions.

questions.q: What does the passage suggest about the US's willingness to use force?
opts: (A) The US is unwilling to use force
(B) The US uses force only in self-defense
(C) The US is willing to use force to achieve its goals
(D) The US never uses force in international relations
questions.explanation: The passage states that the US's actions were seen as a 'clear exercise of its military power and a demonstration of its willingness to use force to achieve its goals'.

questions.q: What was the impact of the US invasion of Iraq on the Iraqi people?
opts: (A) It led to a significant improvement in their living standards
(B) It resulted in the deaths of thousands of Iraqis and the displacement of millions
(C) It had no impact on the Iraqi people
(D) It led to a peaceful transition to democracy
questions.explanation: The passage states that the invasion led to the deaths of thousands of Iraqis and the displacement of millions.

cases.passage: The US's role in shaping the global economic order is another example of its hegemony in world politics. The US has played a key role in the establishment of international economic institutions such as the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund (IMF). These institutions have been instrumental in promoting US economic interests and maintaining the US dollar as a global reserve currency. The US has also used its economic power to shape global trade policies and impose its own economic model on other countries. This has led to criticisms that the US is using its economic power to maintain its dominance in the global economy.

questions.q: What is the role of the World Bank and the IMF in the global economic order?
opts: (A) They promote the economic interests of non-US countries
(B) They are independent institutions that make decisions based on global economic needs
(C) They promote US economic interests and maintain the US dollar as a global reserve currency
(D) They have no role in the global economic order
questions.explanation: The passage states that these institutions have been instrumental in promoting US economic interests and maintaining the US dollar as a global reserve currency.

questions.q: How has the US used its economic power to shape global trade policies?
opts: (A) By imposing tariffs on non-US goods
(B) By using its economic power to shape global trade policies and impose its own economic model on other countries
(C) By reducing trade barriers between countries
(D) By withdrawing from international trade agreements
questions.explanation: The passage states that the US has used its economic power to shape global trade policies and impose its own economic model on other countries.

questions.q: What is the impact of the US's economic model on other countries?
opts: (A) It leads to economic growth and development in non-US countries
(B) It imposes the US economic model on other countries
(C) It has no impact on other countries
(D) It leads to economic instability in non-US countries
questions.explanation: The passage states that the US has used its economic power to impose its own economic model on other countries.

questions.q: What criticism has been leveled against the US's use of its economic power?
opts: (A) That it is too weak to effectively shape global economic policies
(B) That it is too strong and imposing its own economic model on other countries
(C) That it is not using its economic power effectively
(D) That it is not using its economic power at all
questions.explanation: The passage states that the US's use of its economic power has led to criticisms that it is using its economic power to maintain its dominance in the global economy.

questions.q: What is the significance of the US dollar being a global reserve currency?
opts: (A) It means that the US has no control over global economic policies
(B) It means that the US has significant control over global economic policies
(C) It has no significance in the global economic order
(D) It means that the US has no economic power
questions.explanation: The passage states that the US has used its economic power to maintain the US dollar as a global reserve currency.
